metric_value: Kastner worked every summer from age twelve in his family's Cape Cod restaurant, which served more than 1,000 diners a night.
Job was to reconcile tens of thousands of paper slips: every drink, appetizer, bottle of wine traced to a check, matched to a cashier deposit, to the penny.
The Federal Reserve employed half a million people sorting and re-touching checks as they moved through the American banking system.
Commerce was extraordinarily labor intensive; making an airline reservation required a half-hour phone call and a trip to a travel agent to collect a physical ticket.
Before leaving for college, Kastner decided that machines should handle the paper and people should handle everything else.
Information technology is never just about technology—it is about the collision of systems with institutions, economics, and human nature.
Every information system is really an argument with reality.
Kastner's understanding of computing's social role developed gradually at MIT.
notes: Exact years at MIT not stated; estimated from career arc.
